chunk_summary: Explores infection-driven catabolism, gut dysfunction, and recovery strategies in wasting, emphasizing energy needs, EED, and clinical follow-up in low-resource settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of infection and gut dysfunction in acute wasting and recovery challenges.
Learn the clinical significance of appetite changes and their link to unresolved illness or inflammation.
Identify energy requirements and supplementation strategies for children with moderate wasting.
Explore the impact of environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) on nutrient absorption and recovery.
key_facts: Acute wasting results from a mismatch between intake/absorption and physiological needs, amplified by infection–nutrition cycles.
Environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) causes intestinal inflammation, impaired absorption, and barrier disruption, common in low-resource settings.
WHO 2023 guidelines recommend 100–130 kcal/kg/day for children aged 6–59 months with moderate wasting during recovery.
Relapse after discharge often indicates unresolved infection burden, food insecurity, or caregiving constraints.
Counseling should include WASH actions and prompt diarrhoea treatment to reduce gut injury.
reference_quote: Persistent poor appetite is a marker of unresolved illness or inflammation rather than 'behavior,' and should trigger morbidity screening and closer follow-up. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK601642/)
document_key_topics: Energy requirements for recovery in moderate wasting
Clinical follow-up and relapse prevention strategies
